title: One knob to tune them all: Phase-controlled photon statistics and linewidth in partially pumped atomic ensembles
authors: Oksana Chelpanova, Martino Stefanini, Dusan Sarenac, Tim Thomay, Jamir Marino
abstract: We study a minimal model of collective light emission from an incoherently driven ensemble of atoms where incoherent drive is applied only to a part of atoms and show that both the linewidth and the photon statistics can be controlled within a single framework. In this setting, collective dissipation induces correlations between the pumped and unpumped parts of the system, leading to interference between their emission contributions. By introducing a relative phase between these contributions and tuning the pump rate, we demonstrate that the properties of the emitted light can be varied over a broad range. In particular, the linewidth can be made either independent of system size or scale extensively with it, while the photon statistics can be tuned from antibunched or quantum to bunched. We further show that the role of the relative phase in controlling the interference can alternatively be played by the coherent interaction. By tuning the interaction strength together with the pump rate, one can access the same regimes as in the dissipation-only model. In addition, coherent interactions stabilize regimes of coherent emission with narrow linewidth, reminiscent of superradiant lasing. Our results illustrate how interference in partially driven collective systems provides a flexible mechanism for tailoring both spectral and statistical properties of light.
summary: This paper investigates how a relative phase in a partially pumped atomic ensemble can serve as a single control knob for light properties. By tuning this phase and the pump rate, the researchers show that one can transition between quantum (antibunched) and classical (bunched) light while simultaneously controlling the spectral linewidth. The study also demonstrates that coherent interactions can replicate these interference effects and even stabilize superradiant-like lasing. This work offers a flexible framework for designing customized quantum light sources using collective dissipation.
main_problem: The study seeks a versatile method to independently control both the photon statistics (quantum vs. classical) and the spectral linewidth of light emitted from an atomic ensemble.
main_result: The authors demonstrate that a single relative phase between pumped and unpumped atoms acts as a 'knob' to tune light from antibunched to bunched, and from broad to ultra-narrow linewidths.
method: The study uses exact numerical solutions within the Dicke manifold, spectral decomposition of the Liouvillian, and adiabatic elimination for large-scale limits.
model_or_system: A minimal model of an ensemble of N+1 two-level atoms where one atom is incoherently pumped and the rest undergo collective decay, described by a Lindblad master equation with a phase-dependent jump operator.
key_observables: Photon statistics (g2, g3), spectral function S(omega), linewidth (delta nu), magnetization (Sz), and light intensity.
important_parameters: Relative phase (phi), pump rate (w), collective decay rate (Gamma), and coherent interaction strength (V).
main_assumptions: The analysis primarily focuses on the maximal J subspace (Dicke states) and assumes a Lindblad master equation framework.
figures_summary: Fig 1 shows the setup and emission pathways; Fig 3/4 illustrates various regimes of linewidth and photon statistics; Fig 6 summarizes the relationship between observables like magnetization, intensity, and coherence.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the partially pumped model, analyzes the phase-controlled interference mechanism, explores the scaling of linewidth and statistics, extends the model to include coherent interactions, and discusses experimental implementation via cavity QED.
why_interesting: It provides a novel mechanism for engineering light sources where dissipation and interference are used as resources to tailor quantum optical properties and stabilize narrow-linewidth emission.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. (a) Schematic of the setup. A single spin σ is incoherently pumped at rate w and decays collectively together with the collective spin J. (b) Illustration of the two pathways in Liouvillian processes by which excitations are transferred from the pump to the environment: path 1 corresponds to excitation and subsequent emission by the pumped atom, while path 2 corresponds to transfer of the excitation to the unpumped spin J, followed by emission into the environment. (c) Spectral function of a single atom driven to the steady state by incoherent pumping w and loss Γ (black line), the spectral function for the setup in panel (a) (red line), and the spectral function for the same setup...

FIG. 2. Spectral decomposition of S (ω) for a system of two spins. (a) Real and (b) imaginary parts of the Liouvillian eigenvalues for a system of 1 + 1 spins. The real part of the slowest eigenvalue λ1 saturates at the value −Γ/2, while the other eigenvalues decrease linearly with the pump rate w. (c–h) Contributions to the spectral function from different Liouvillian eigenmodes for w = 5Γ, using Eq. (5). In the symmetric case (c), the eigenvalue λ1 contributes to S (ω) with a negative sign, and the linewidth is determined by | Re λ2|. In the antisymmetric case (g), the interference is constructive and the linewidth is proportional to the real part of the slowest eigenvalue | Re λ1|. For...

FIG. 3. Properties of the light emitted by an ensemble of 101 atoms. (a) Normalized spectral function ˜S (ω) = S (ω)/ max(S (ω)) for different values of the phase ϕ, with the pump rate fixed at w = ΓN. (b) Intensity, (c) second- and (d) third-order coherence, (e) linewidth, and (f) position of the largest peak (line shift) as functions of the phase ϕ and pump rate w. For ϕ close to π the emitted light is always quantum and g(2)(0) ∝1/N, while for ϕ ≈0 the photon statistics can be tuned by varying w, with g(2)(0) ∝NΓ/w. (g) Higher-order coherences as a function of w for different k and ϕ = 0. (h) Schematic of the accessible regimes summarizing the scaling of g(2)(0) and ∆ν. By tuning w and...

FIG. 4. Properties of the light emitted by an ensemble of 21 atoms in the presence of coherent interaction V. Steady-state (a) magnetization, (b) intensity, (c) second- and (d) third-order coherence, (e) linewidth, and (f) position of the peak in the spectral function as functions of the pump rate w and interaction strength V. (g) Diagram summarizing the regimes with bunched light g(2)(0) > 1 and ultra-narrow ∆ν < 2Γ (B UN), narrow ∆ν < ΓN (B N), broad ∆ν > ΓN (B B) linewidth, classical (or coherent) light g(2)(0) →1 with ultra-narrow (C UN, superradiant lasing regime), narrow (C N) and broad (C B) linewidth and quantum light g(2)(0) < 1 with ultranarrow (Q UN), narrow (Q N) and broad (Q B)...
FIG. 5. Normalized spectral function for system of 21 spins gov- erned by model (C1) as a function of (a) ϕ for w = ΓN and (b) as a function of pump rate for ϕ = 0.

FIG. 6. Properties of the light emitted by an ensemble of 21 atoms governed by model (C1). Steady-state (a) magnetization, (b) intensity, (c) second-order coherence, (d) third-order coherence, (e) linewidth, and (f) position of the peak in the spectral function as functions of the pump rate w and the phase ϕ. (g) Dependence of the k-th order coherence on the pump rate for different orders k and ϕ = π. The light displays thermal statistics in the bunched regime. (h) Diagram summarizing the regimes with bunched light g(2)(0) > 1 and ultra-narrow linewidth ∆ν < 2Γ (B UN), bunched light with narrow linewidth ∆ν < ΓN (B N), bunched light with broad linewidth ∆ν > ΓN (B B), and quantum...

FIG. 7. Normalized spectral functions for the partially pumped system with coherent interaction. Normalized spectral functions for model (8) as a function of pump rate (a–c) and interaction strength (d–f). Notice, that ˜S (ω) in panels (d) and (e) is plotted in logarithmic scale to emphasize fine details.
